# **Thoughts on Merlin Backfill**

Status: *Under review*  Author: [Yi Chen](mailto:yichen@stripe.com)    Created: Sep 23, 2024  Last Updated: Oct 9, 2024

| Aug 6, 2024 | Shared the i[nitial concern](https://slack-pde.slack.com/archives/CK4C1AWNA/p1722963674951029?thread_ts=1722448820.151809&cid=CK4C1AWNA) about merlin backfill design, and an [initial suggestion](https://slack-pde.slack.com/archives/CK4C1AWNA/p1722979180976619?thread_ts=1722448820.151809&cid=CK4C1AWNA) for a fix. |
| :---- | :---- |
| Oct 7, 2024 | [Yi Chen](mailto:chen.y@salesforce.com)[Aaron Keys](mailto:akeys@salesforce.com)[Juan Calvert Barba](mailto:jcalvert@salesforce.com)[Sherin Thomas](mailto:sherin.thomas@salesforce.com)[Nedra Albrecht](mailto:nalbrecht@salesforce.com) met for a meeting to discuss the details of Merlin. [Aaron Keys](mailto:akeys@salesforce.com)followed up with governance recommendations for Merlin ([slack thread](https://slack-pde.slack.com/archives/C03185TA0UU/p1728339436739089)). |
| Oct 7, 2024 | Confirmed with [Aaron Keys](mailto:akeys@salesforce.com)that the root cause of incident [\#incd-240408-51148-merlin-jobs-are-failing-on-prod-dma-clus](https://slack-pde.slack.com/archives/C06SWV6JSKH) was the Merlin jobs not following the best practice and hitting limits on Spark. See [slack thread](https://slack-pde.slack.com/archives/C03DN0TL77Z/p1728338609650209?thread_ts=1715187616.709589&cid=C03DN0TL77Z).  |
| Oct 10, 2024 | Discussed in the reliability workgroup meeting. Shared in the [backfill proposal thread](https://slack-pde.slack.com/archives/CK4C1AWNA/p1728585670520359?thread_ts=1722448820.151809&cid=CK4C1AWNA). |
| Oct 14, 2024 | Shared my idea of a [potential high-level proposal](https://docs.google.com/document/d/17v4yTONsVQNWuackMUDMwRKqKCb9OpEpZIqFJRkYf1A/edit?tab=t.0) for a fix that can solve scalability and reliability challenges at the same time. |
| Oct 17, 2024 | Met with stakeholders, we agreed that the DI team will stop supporting merlin backfill failures from \#escal-data channel. Merlin team will be responsible for merlin backfill reliability and debugging. DI team will support incidents (merlin team will be responsible for leading the incidents and post-mortems. See [announcements](https://slack-pde.slack.com/archives/CJFHE7AR3/p1729270014685799?thread_ts=1727120217.112089&cid=CJFHE7AR3). |

# Summary

During my investigation of a Merlin backfill [escal](#2024-09-16-escal:-ml-team-have-been-exploring-using-merlin-to-calculate-aggregated-signals-as-features-for-ranking), I noticed a pattern consistent with previous escalations and incidents, for which I have raised a concern in August. In this escal, we were able to remove many environmental noises that made it difficult to root-cause before. That is, the backfill was running in a dedicated backfill cluster, backfill-emr6, without spot instances. We used the most powerful EC2 machines available for us (on-demand r7g.16xlarge) and equipped them with the largest disk spaces in our infrastructure (4TB per machine). This enabled me to identify the root cause of the Merlin backfill issues. I believe there is a design flaw in Merlin’s backfill process, that makes it both unreliable and unscalable. 

I believe there is an increasing reliability risk for Merlin jobs as we start to optimize Spark jobs to [rightsize the executors](https://docs.google.com/document/d/199wkuvnvGWK5-5qdq83baCbvKaX3H_WiFwjJJXHDwY0/) from FY25H2 (see our [planning doc and scope](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1nWRwbMCN2qTebxF1CdVqy6xBMemLOs58Lz1s9E3F17g/edit)). After merlin backfills are moved to a dedicated cluster, the [application success rate is below 5%](#bookmark=id.rhf52apcfkd1). Given the design and risk, I think the merlink backfill failures are [not supportable from the infrastructure perspective](#bookmark=id.8iq6d83mxr47). 

To facilitate cross-team discussion, I'll begin by explaining my understanding of Merlin's operation and the known root causes of reliability issues from an infrastructure perspective. I'll then discuss potential impacts based on my understanding of Merlin's design and ongoing investigations supporting that hypothesis. During the cross-team discussion, I aim to gain a deeper understanding of Merlin's internal mechanics and current improvement efforts. The goal is to determine if my two primary concerns are already being addressed. These concerns are:

1. The impact of Merlin backfill on the infrastructure’s reliability, the landing time of key datasets. I observed that Merlin backfill will exhaust disk spaces on the machines and result in killing a large number of machines in the cluster, which impacts the cluster reliability, causing delays of key datasets, and increase of cluster costs.    
2. The absence of robust processes and tooling to validate Merlin jobs (and any SQL-based jobs), intended to simplify data job creation for end-users, ultimately became a significant reliability concern for the Spark infrastructure. This tends to generate unmanageable workloads for the Data Infrastructure team.

# How Merlin Backfill Works {#how-merlin-backfill-works}

I recently reached out to the Merlin team to help understand how the backfill works. According to [Merlin experts](https://slack-pde.slack.com/archives/C03185TA0UU/p1727115304869599):

* Each Merlin metric is defined declaratively with a templated SQL query. This query contains placeholders for `start_date` and `end_date`. Merlin controls the values of these two parameters. The user also specifies a `start_date`, which determines the amount of backfill required during the initial run.  
* Merlin does not differentiate between daily runs and backfills. Each day, the Merlin job checks the declared `start_date` against the current date to determine if a backfill is necessary. If no backfill is required, the job sets `start_date` and `end_date` to the current date and processes data for that single day.  
* When a backfill is required, Merlin processes the data in batches determined by the `backfill_batch_interval` parameter. For example, if a 365-day backfill is needed, it will process 14 days of data at a time. This process is internal to the job, meaning that if a 14-day batch is successful, the same Spark job will continue with the next 14-day batch until the entire backfill is complete.  
* When `backfill_batch_interval` is set to 1 day and a backfill is required, Merlin will process the data one day at a time in a serialized manner. For example, if a 90-day backfill is needed, it will first backfill the earliest day's data. Only after completing the backfill for the first day will it proceed to the second day's data.  
* If a merlin job fails, Airflow will retry it up to 4 times, and each retry will repeat the same logic above.

# Symptoms of unhealthy Backfill

From past escals and incident investigation, I have noticed a few main symptoms when it comes to backfill. They are:

- The backfill jobs with issues tend to show a large number of stage-level retries.  
- The backfill tends to take a very long time, up to multiple weeks.  
- There is a perception that there are not enough cluster resources for running backfills  
- There are symptoms of cluster reliability when large backfills are unhealthy.  
- The unhealthy backfill jobs tend to have large disk spill   
- We tend to see backfill jobs failing with exceeding the limit of 2000 lost executors

Previous efforts primarily focused on debugging specific job failures assuming the Merlin jobs are typical ETL-style Spark jobs. I debugged several before gaining a comprehensive understanding of Merlin backfill. Due to the extensive nature of the Spark infrastructure, we've encountered a wide range of failure modes, including shuffle failures, task retry limit failures, lost executors over the limit, and others. While debugging these issues, I initially assumed I was working with a standard ETL-style Spark job. However, now that I understand Merlin backfill, I realize that not only were previous efforts misguided, but my past debugging efforts might inadvertently cause future problems, hence the urgency of a cross-team discussion. I believe the Merlink backfill failures are [not debuggable](#bookmark=id.b0a682ra2s8h) and [not fixable](#bookmark=id.6przt2ewvsdd) without a redesign. 

Most of the previous effort was focusing on debugging particular backfill job failure. I personally debugged a few before I understood [how merlin backfill works](#how-merlin-backfill-works). Because the surface area of Spark infrastructure is large, we have seen a large number of different types of failure modes, including shuffle failure, task retry limit failure, etc. I have debugged many of these situations assuming I am debugging a common ETL style Spark job. Now that I understand [how merlin backfill works](#how-merlin-backfill-works), I realized that all previous efforts not only are going in the wrong direction, my previous debugging efforts may cause more issues down the road, hence the urgency of the cross-team discussion.

# Root Cause Analysis

Merlin backfill allows one Spark job designed for processing one-day worth of data, to handle multiple days worth of data. By default, it means that each daily Spark job will be fed with 14-day worth of data. Furthermore, this size is unpredictable, because it can be changed by a configuration `backfill_batch_interval`. This creates unpredictable and unknown risks not only for the Merlin jobs themselves, it also creates unpredictable risks for the clusters and all the jobs running on them. 

Spark was created to replace Hadoop MapReduce, and its original design purpose was to handle ETL jobs. Spark’s in-memory processing is significantly faster than MapReduce’s disk-based processing. ETL jobs typically assume that daily or hourly jobs have predictable data size and shape. Because of this consistency and predictability, we have the ability to tune each individual job based on its own profile, and make the right trade off tuning decision to find the trade-off between performance, cost, and reliability. This consistency and predictability is determined by the specific job logic (e.g. the SQL query), and the size and shape of data input (e.g. regular daily inputs). Usually we anticipate some head room during tuning to consider organic data size growth.

One would wonder why we cannot use one spark job designed for one day to handle multiple days worth of data.  I will provide some speculations to explain why this could happen here in the  [appendix](#bookmark=id.wpk12qabfzvg). I think this practice is risky because it can have unintended consequences. Please see [the appendix regarding potential risks](#bookmark=id.uhsv6jqe71bc). 

| *Imagine daily, predictable jobs as boxes on a conveyor belt moving at a constant speed, each containing a day's worth of data. If the content size and shape is predictable, we can optimize the box size and shape so the content can be packed neatly and also minimize the size and shape of the box for max throughput. We can also optimize the weight-bearing mechanism of the conveyor belt, the funnel dimensions (the infrastructure) to ensure that everything works as expected without large waste. During Merlin backfill, we essentially combine the content of 14 boxes into one, removing 13 boxes per batch and accelerating the backfill process. However, this requires several assumptions about the conveyor belt. For instance, we must assume that a single box can hold 14 days' worth of content without spilling out of the box, and that even if spills occur, the box can navigate all stages and tunnels over the belt and pipeline safely. Additionally, the conveyor belt itself must be able to handle the increased weight density. By reusing the existing conveyor belt, we avoid requesting additional resources for the denser workload, so the scalability is fundamentally limited to the existing one belt. We may have multiple conveyor belts in the same warehouse available, but the Merlin backfill will not be able to use them.*  |
| :---- |

We can attempt to explain the impact of the Merlin backfill design:

* The backfill job cannot use the cluster resources because it is only requesting for the resources based on the daily-job. Merlin backfill jobs are using the same daily job Spark application, which will not request more cluster resources for the backill. If a user wants to use the daily job to backfill 365 days of data, it will use the same cluster resources of a daily job. I was able to confirm that we can predict with accuracy on how many machine resources the backfill will request based on the configurations.  
* I can confirm that Merlin backfill consumes large amounts of disk space. As a result, it will exhaust many machine’s disk spaces and eventually lose those machines. As a result, all applications running on those machines will be affected, and it will force stage-level retries for all affected applications to regenerate the lost state. We currently don’t fully understand why Merlin jobs can consume such big amounts of disk space. I will address the potential reasons and ongoing investigations in the next section.  
* We now know why in the incident, we saw the Merlin backfill failure after the lost executors reaching the limit of 2000\. It is because the Merlin backfill job itself (or other merlin backfill jobs in the same cluster) are taking out machines, resulting in lost executors on those machines. Also because the Merlin backfill job is a long-running job, it simply accumulates such count during its lifetime.   
* Now that merlin backfills are running in a dedicated cluster, we have a better understanding of its reliability metrics. The current data shows that [less than 5% of applications are successful in the backfill-emr6 cluster](#bookmark=id.rhf52apcfkd1). This is because Merlin backfill is using one application and expecting it to finish the whole backfill dataset, be it 365 days, or 90 days. It is risky to design the system to anticipate failure without impacting the infrastructure.  

# Investigations

To illustrate my hypothesis about how Merlin backfill works with an analogy:

| *Imagine a regular conveyor belt pipeline. There's a machine responsible for packing daily content into a box and loading it onto the belt. Once this process is complete, the machine discards the box. Let's call this machine Airflow.  There's another machine responsible for packing 14 days' worth of content into a single box and loading it onto the belt. Unlike the first machine, this one never discards the box. Instead, it catches the box after each pass, immediately reuses it, and packs the next batch of 14-day content. This machine is named MerlinBackfill. It assumes that the box can be reused until the final batch of content is processed. In this analogy, each box represents an application, and the content is the data input for daily runs with specific size and shape. Airflow runs each application for one day of data, and upon application completion, all application state is cleared. During backfill, it seems that the same Spark application is expected not only to process a larger batch of content but also to remain active as a service, continuously receiving more content until the backfill is finished.* |
| :---- |

If this analogy is accurate, it may help surface an area of concern. For instance, we haven't fully grasped the consequences of very long-running Spark jobs with continuous batches of large input data volumes. While Spark jobs are commonly used for ETL, not as services (except in Spark Streaming with micro batches), long-running Spark jobs can potentially accumulate significant log data that may not be cleaned up until the application exits. We're also uncertain about other disk-based states that might accumulate during the long life-cycle. See [DI-7410](https://jira.tinyspeck.com/browse/DI-7410).

### *Rootcausing **\#incd-240408-51148-merlin-jobs-are-failing-on-prod-dma-clus***

We should also revisit the incident **\#incd-240408-51148-merlin-jobs-are-failing-on-prod-dma-clus** that forced us to create a dedicated cluster prod-temp-dma-emr6. We were able to confirm with a SQL query that it can trigger an internal regression bug in Spark. The bug is a special corner case that only impacts some very rare large Spark jobs. See [https://issues.apache.org/jira/browse/SPARK-47897](https://issues.apache.org/jira/browse/SPARK-47897). During the follow-up investigation, I noticed that the failed job is pretty large, with multiple levels of subqueries and aggregation in them. With Aaron’s comment regarding the merlin job (see [in slack](https://slack-pde.slack.com/archives/C01FB307Z7A/p1728078353314279?thread_ts=1726513687.137119&cid=C01FB307Z7A)), I suspect that the failed job may not follow the best practices for merlin jobs which triggered rare corner case limits in Spark. Hence, we should revisit this incident from two angles:

- Confirm that this job failed because it is doing backfill. Confirm that this job can run successfully without doing backfill on EMR 6.12. (confirmed, see the [thread in the incident channel](https://slack-pde.slack.com/archives/C06SWV6JSKH/p1712631004483879))  
- Confirm with Aaron that the query is following Merlin's best practice. 

We also need to revisit the reliability of the DEA cluster. I observed that the DEA cluster runs merlin jobs, but [this cluster has created an unreasonable amount of support requests, and many of their jobs were wasting 99% of the requested resources](https://slack-pde.slack.com/archives/C8EDM9MGA/p1715706288953739?thread_ts=1713372562.786779&cid=C8EDM9MGA). We should confirm that their Merlin jobs are over-sized, and their jobs are following the recommended best practices.

### *DMA clusters Disk Usage*

The DMA cluster used to experience frequent disk space issues (examples: [escal](https://slack-pde.slack.com/archives/C01FB307Z7A/p1715724355520159?thread_ts=1715442922.922879&cid=C01FB307Z7A)), which was visible from grafana charts. I noticed that recently the DMA cluster disk usage has become stable and healthy. See the following grafana chart ([link](https://grafana.tinyspeck.com/d/BiIDMnAZz/emr?orgId=1&from=now-7d&to=now&var-cluster=prod-dma-emr6&var-role=All&var-interval=10m)) showing the past 7 days of disk usage. It is possible that those disk consumption issues we saw in the past were caused by merlin backfills, because we now know that merlin backfills will only request a small amount of machines, but consume large amounts of disk space on them. We will continue to monitor this situation.

![][image1]

# Case Studies

## 2024-09-16 escal: ML team have been exploring using merlin to calculate aggregated signals as features for ranking {#2024-09-16-escal:-ml-team-have-been-exploring-using-merlin-to-calculate-aggregated-signals-as-features-for-ranking}

See the full context in this thread in escal channel: [https://slack-pde.slack.com/archives/C01FB307Z7A/p1726513687137119](https://slack-pde.slack.com/archives/C01FB307Z7A/p1726513687137119)

### *Use Case*

This is a case of a new merlin job. The user is onboarding new metrics and they want to backfill 1 year (365 days) of data. Because this is a new use case, most configurations are the default configuration. By default, a new Merlin job will use a small executor of 4GB/1 vcore. 

### *Cluster Environment*

The backfill-emr6 cluster is configured to use **r7g.16xlarge** machines, the best machine AWS has for our workload. We are also using **4TB of EBS volume for each such machine**, which doubles the disk spaces for non-backfill clusters (e.g. prod-dma-emr6 and prod-data-platform-emr6). Most importantly, **this cluster is only using on-demand instances**: there are no spot-instances during the life cycle of this backfill.

![][image2]

### *Scalability Limitation* {#scalability-limitation}

I’ve noticed that during backfill, the stage-level parallelism is capped at 1000\. It is still not clear where this configuration is set. One possibility is that it comes from an AQE initial partition count. We need to confirm this setting. This cap limits the cluster resources the job can request. 

In this backfill escal, each backfill can consume at most 16 machines. During the lifetime of the backfill, I noticed that the live nodes are always around 30, way lower than the total available cluster resources. This can be explained by the screenshot below. It shows that there are only two backfill jobs running in the cluster. Because each job is running 1000 partitions, each partition consumes one CPU core, and we are running r7g.16xlarge machines which provide 64 cores, each job only needs 16 machines to run (1000/64=16), so the two jobs only need about 30 machines to run.. 

![][image3]

### *The Job is killing the cluster*

Note the screenshot above, that with two backfill jobs, the cluster only has **30** live nodes, but it has **364** lost nodes. That is, [this backfill dag managed to have killed 10x of machines for 5 days so far.](https://slack-pde.slack.com/archives/C01FB307Z7A/p1727128172486099?thread_ts=1726513687.137119&cid=C01FB307Z7A) When backfilling a different table, I observed the [killing rate of 14 machines in 17 minutes](https://slack-pde.slack.com/archives/C01FB307Z7A/p1726798210177129?thread_ts=1726513687.137119&cid=C01FB307Z7A). The backfill job is [killing machines by overrun the disk space](https://slack-pde.slack.com/archives/C01FB307Z7A/p1726797015251659?thread_ts=1726513687.137119&cid=C01FB307Z7A). 

The backfill job is killing the cluster, by consuming too much disk space on the machines. As a result, YARN will mark those nodes with more than 90% disk utilization as UNHEALTHY (out of 4TB space per machine), and eventually those nodes are marked as lost because the RM will lose contact with them. The following screenshot shows that all the UNHEALTHY nodes are caused by running of disk spaces ([see in escal context](https://slack-pde.slack.com/archives/C01FB307Z7A/p1726797015251659?thread_ts=1726513687.137119&cid=C01FB307Z7A)).

![][image4]

# Appendix

**Q: We have seen improved backfill speed by sending multiple-days worth of data to the daily job. How do you explain that?**

This can be explained by a few different angles. Firstly, it is possible that the daily job resources are oversized. For example, we have seen jobs that are wasting 99% of the resources it requested. Because these jobs are so oversized, it can handle more data without requesting more resources. This can explain that a large number of jobs in DMA clusters are oversized, and when they are in backfill mode, we can expect a certain level of performance gain.

Another possibility is that the stage-level setting of 1000 (see details [here](#scalability-limitation)). I am not sure where this number comes from. It is possible that this same configuration also applies for daily runs, but because of the lower volume of daily runs, they won’t be able to saturate this capped maximum. Because backfill processes more data, it allows them to reach this capped maximum, which allows the job to request more cluster resources than its daily runs.

**Q: What are the potential risks of feeding multiple-days worth of data to the daily job?**

Spark jobs internally operate by first listing all input files, discovering and recording metadata (including file count and sizes), and formulating a logical and physical execution plan based on the job's logic. This plan can be adjusted in response to changes in data size or shape, and with Adaptive Query Execution (AQE), the plan can even change during execution. For example, a particularly small table might be identified as fitting in memory, leading Spark to load it in the driver and broadcast it to all executors. This significantly improves performance and accelerates job completion. However, dynamic changes also require more execution memory to store broadcast datasets, which cannot be spilled to disk. Consequently, this dynamic nature increases the risk of Out-of-Memory (OOM) errors in both the driver and executors.

Spark's dynamic execution path based on input can explain a common user confusion that users tend to attribute to the infrastructure: jobs that have run successfully for extended periods may suddenly fail due to changes in input data, even though the application source code hasn’t changed. 

Although Spark has a large surface area, as long as the fundamental assumption that the job is processing predictable size and shape of input, these failures are still relatively easier to debug. However, Merlin backfill allows each Spark job designed for a daily run (or hourly run?) to process a much larger volume, it breaks the assumption, and creates a situation that the backfill is not debuggable and fixable. The reason is the following:

- The `backfill_batch_interval` is a changeable configuration. If we tune the job to meet a particular configuration, when this value is changed, it becomes a totally new job with a totally new risk profile. This creates a moving target that is impossible to get right.   
- If we ever fixed one unhealthy backfill job configured to be bigger than 1, it means we will be using the new configuration to run its daily normal runs. I don’t consider this a reliability problem, but a cost problem. Essentially, we would be tuning a job to handle 14-day worth of data, to run 1-day worth of data every day. 

**Q: What if we just increase the disk space on the machines so they are more reliable?**

Assuming that we have an ability to tune the jobs so it will be successful without failures, even when it creates unpredictable consumption of local disk space, it still significantly impacts the performance of these jobs. We know that disk spills have a write amplification factor of 3, which means that it is a performance killer. A Spark job with significant disk spills defeats the design goal of using its in-memory compute engine to provide much better performance than disk-based MapReduce. Also, we do not fully understand the consequences of a long-running Merlink backfill job, that expects a single Spark application to process continuously for a huge amount of data across an extended period of time. It is possible that the single application needs to accumulate logs and other temporary files across its lifetime, resulting in more machines being killed by exhausting the disk space. We need to fix the root cause. 

**Q: Why are Merlin Backfill failures not debuggable?**

Merlin backfill will use one Spark application, and iterate through multiple batches, and each batch contains multiple days of data. This batch size is configurable, which creates a moving target for debugging.

Because Merlin sends by batches, there is no way to debug the job using Spark UI. Because the data volume is unpredictable, Spark may generate different query plans, which create different stages based on the current configuration. There is no way to tell from the Spark UI which batch, or which day’s backfill failed and why.

There is no way to tell which jobs or stages belong to what dataset from Spark UI. There is no way to know which dataset is triggering the failure. There is no way to match with the daily run jobs because with different dataset, Spark may generate different logical and physical query plan, resulting in different set of jobs and stages in Spark UI.

**Q: Why are Merlin Backfill failures not fixable?**

Please refer to the answers [why these failures are not debuggable](#bookmark=id.b0a682ra2s8h). Most of our previous effort to try to fix a failed merlin backfill would have resulted in over provisioned jobs. This may explain why we have many unreliable jobs that also happen to be oversized: because we upsized the containers during early backfill failures. Any attempt to fix such backfill failures would result in more waste and cause reliability issues for the daily jobs in other clusters. This is because the fix for backfill failure will result in tuning the job to handle a much larger dataset than its daily runs. When the backfill finishes and the job continues to run in its normal cluster, it will consume much more cluster resources (but most will be wasted), which will cause resource contention and reliability issues in the cluster. 

**Q: Why are Merlin Backfills not supportable from the infrastructure perspective?**

Please refer to the above explanation on why it is not possible to  [debuggable](#bookmark=id.b0a682ra2s8h) and [not fixable](#bookmark=id.6przt2ewvsdd) from tuning the jobs.  When a merlin backfill job fails, the first step is to ask the user to consult with the Merlin team to optimize and rewrite their jobs. However, if the job poses a significant SLA risk, we must declare an incident. This involves all relevant teams, including Merlin, to develop a plan. This approach ensures that we address the immediate issue while also temporary tuning changes can be reverted once the incident is resolved.

**Q: Merlin / SqlPen have been used heavily for years now and this is the first time this has come up as a major issue. Has it always been a problem that we are just now noticing, or has something happened that has caused more incidents recently?**

See [response in context](https://slack-pde.slack.com/archives/C06G45LGG7M/p1728675275195909?thread_ts=1728581436.420909&cid=C06G45LGG7M). Our EMR Spark infrastructure has experienced significant reliability problems. This led both the infrastructure team and users to question EMR's reliability, which I have not seen any evidence so far. Since March, we've identified and resolved many of these issues. For example, we've eliminated spot instances from critical clusters (see spot or not), discovered and fixed a long-standing bug that exhausted file descriptors and affected all jobs on those machines,  We've identified and fixed a shuffle reliability issue (shuffle reliability doc). All of these issues have a larger blast radius than the Merlin backfill. After all of them were fixed, the merlin backfill failures became more apparent, especially when I analyzed all reliability issues from escals at the end of Q2 (Spark Reliability Progress). The decision to move all merlin backfill to an isolated cluster created the opportunity to root-cause this issue.

**Q: How reliable are Merlin Backfills?**

The following chart ([link](https://analytics.tinyspeck.com/v3/dashboard/1000000001475734)) shows the application success rate by cluster. The backfill cluster is at the bottom, usually less than 5%. 

![][image5]

[image3]: <data:image/png;base64,>

[image4]: <data:image/png;base64,>

[image5]: <data:image/png;base64,>
